"The stock's valuation has gotten ahead of the fundamentals," the analysts say. "We have no way to know if a deal will ultimately materialize." Warner Bros. Discovery is off 1.5% to $19.47 premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0547 ET - Taiwan's decision to restrict chip exports to South Africa, which has since been paused, could alarm other countries, economists at Capital Economics say in a note. Taiwan suspended the restriction on Thursday, two days after the initial order, as the two sides agreed to discuss a dispute regarding Taiwan's representative office in the African country. "The threat raises questions about whether such curbs should now be considered part of Taiwan's armory in managing diplomatic ties," they say. Taiwan's dominant role in the semiconductor supply chain gives it some negotiating power, they say. Although Taiwan has paused the export ban, the perception that Taiwan might take advantage of its advanced chips for diplomatic goals could urge countries to reduce their dependence on Taiwanese chips in the future, the economists add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0515 ET - DBS Group Research turns bullish on Baidu, citing strong growth potential in its cloud and robotaxi businesses. The analysts expect Baidu's robotaxi unit to expand rapidly, noting its Wuhan operations have reached operating breakeven on a unit basis. They forecast its cloud business revenue to see on-year growth of 27% in 2025 and 20% in 2026, supported by strong AI demand. Meanwhile, its advertising business is undergoing an AI-driven revamp and could stabilize in 4Q. DBS upgrades Baidu to buy from hold, raising its ADR target to US$177.00 from US$85.00 and its Hong Kong target to HK$175.00 from HK$84.00. Baidu's ADRs closed at US$135.40, and its Hong Kong-listed shares ended at HK$129.10. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0323 ET - Astro Malaysia's operating environment remains challenging, Affin Hwang IB analysts write in a note. The satellite TV operator's introduction of "Astro One" bundles has slowed subscriber churn, among factors, signaling some early success in product bundling. However, the average revenue per user continues to fall at a faster pace, raising concerns over the sustainability of revenue and profitability even if subscriber losses moderate, the analysts say. Astro's radio and television advertising expenditure also remains soft. This suggests muted consumer and business sentiment, as U.S. tariffs and global trade uncertainties weigh on spending, the analysts say. Affin Hwang IB cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.08 from MYR0.13, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares areflat at MYR0.14.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0123 ET - Inari Amertron's earnings outlook seems more positive to MBSB Research as its radio frequency segment utilization rate rises following new smartphone launches, which supports steady revenue. The recent joint acquisition of Lumiled could diversify the company's earnings and tap into global LED and MicroLED growth, although regulatory approval is pending, it says in a note. The company's efforts to expand advanced packaging solutions could accelerate with government fiscal support. However, significant results are likely to be seen in FY2027 rather than FY2026, it adds. MBSB raises Inari's target price to MYR2.12 from MYR1.98, while maintaining a neutral rating on the stock. Shares are 1.4% lower at MYR2.12. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0048 ET - Life360's bulls at Morgan Stanley don't think that the location-app developer's price fully reflects the prospects for its new pet-tracking service. The MS analysts see potential upside in subscriber growth, retention and revenue from the bundling of the service to 11 million dog-owning families among Life360's user base. They also think that Life360 could launch new partnerships with third parties that increase the utility of its platform. They say in a note that they are looking forward to the dual-listed company's 3Q results in November and news on early take-up of the pet service. MS lifts its target price on Life360's Australia-listed stock 15% to A$58.50 and stays overweight. Shares are up 3.7% at A$52.87. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0041 ET - UBTech Robotics has a bright outlook with rising capacity and sales momentum, Citi analysts say in a note. The company will boost its humanoid robot capacity to about 300 units a month by end-September, enough to support 2026 deliveries, the analysts say, citing management. UBTech expects to deliver more than 500 units in 2025 and 2,000-3,000 units in 2026, backed by strong intent orders across industries. Citi says the company has a first-mover advantage in industrial applications. Citi lifts its 2025 and 2026 revenue forecasts by 5% and 3%, upgrades the stock to buy from buy/high risk and raises its target price to HK$170.00 from HK$121.00. Shares last traded at HK$139.30. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2325 ET - Tencent is likely to benefit from the recent surge in game approvals by Chinese authorities, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su write in a note. Su forecasts Tencent's revenue to grow 13.1% in 2025 and 10.4% in 2026 and adjusted EPS to rise 20.1% this year. The analyst notes that Chinese authorities approved 463 game licenses in 3Q, a 34% on-year increase and a 23% year-to-date rise through September, suggesting the Chinese gaming industry is on track for double-digit growth in the coming quarters. Morningstar views Tencent as undervalued, trading at a 20% discount to its valuation, maintaining its fair value estimates of $102.00 on Tencent's ADR, which last closed at $83.31. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2310 ET - TSMC's 2025 revenue could beat its already lifted full-year guidance of 30% growth in U.S. dollar terms amid rising AI demand, Citi analysts say in a research note. TSMC could see even stronger demand for its advanced chips into 2027 thanks to the bigger integrated-circuit size for the next-generation chips and expected strong 2-nanometer chip ramp-up starting from 2H 2026, they say. TSMC's capex could reach US$47 billion and US$50 billion in 2026 and 2027, respectively, they say. Citi thinks TSMC will like reallocate more of its research and design resources and capacity to the most advanced nodes and advanced packaging. Maintaining a buy call on TSMC, Citi raises its target price to NT$1,600.00 from NT$1,400.00. Shares were recently at NT$1,300.00. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0840 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs came with an exception for companies building new factories in the U.S., but short-term levies could still be faced if projects aren't under way by Oct. 1, Panmure Liberum analysts write. Britain's GSK and AstraZeneca have announced major investments in the U.S., which might shield them, they write. However, those programs won't launch until 2026, a deadline that may expose the groups to short-term levies, the analysts say. Meanwhile, the announcement was encouraging for phrama groups that focus on generic drugs, as they appear to be exempt from tariff plans, they add. Hikma and Sandoz shares are up 1.7% and 1.3%, respectively, while GSK and AstraZeneca are steady. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0813 ET - New U.S. drug tariffs shouldn't affect Belgian pharma group UCB as the company outlined plans to build a manufacturing site in the country, ING analysts write. ING says that, as far as it is aware, the 15% ceiling on U.S. levies on imports from the EU applies to branded pharmaceuticals under the recent U.S.-EU tariff deal, the analysts say. Moreover, 65% of UCB's products are manufactured by third parties worldwide, they add. UCB said in June that it would build a manufacturing plant in the U.S. that would create 300 permanent and 500 construction jobs. UCB shares rise 0.8% and are up 3.1% year-on-year. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0757 ET - It is unclear how new U.S. drug tariffs relate to a U.S.-EU trade deal capping levies, Novo Nordisk CEO Mike Doustdar says. The Danish pharma group looks forward to working with the Trump administration, but it is uncertain whether the earlier deal between the U.S. and the EU will keep tariffs at 15% rather than the 100% announced by President Trump, Doustdar writes in a statement. Construction is under way on a Novo Nordisk manufacturing site in North Carolina, he adds. Trump announced Thursday that the tariffs would hit pharmaceutical companies not building U.S. plants. Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.7%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0707 ET - A German business group representing chemical and pharmaceutical companies is urging the EU to push for an agreement with President Trump on tariffs for medicines. VCI says it is very concerned about Trump's plan to impose an 100% tariff on pharmaceutical imports as of October. The move comes after the U.S. and the EU struck a deal this summer for a 15% U.S. tariff on medicines coming from the bloc. VCI CEO Wolfgang Grosse Entrup says the tariff announcement is a low point for EU-U.S. trade relations. The U.S. is an important market for the German pharmaceutical industry, with exports amounting to 27.9 billion euros in 2024, he adds. The Stoxx Europe 600 Health Care index is up 0.1%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0409 ET - U.S. President Trump's new round of sectoral tariff are likely to negatively affect highly trade-dependent economies like Singapore, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. The measures, including a 100% tariff on branded or patented drug imports from Oct. 1, marks a continuation of Trump's protectionist agenda, he says. "Continued imposition of U.S. tariffs and ongoing uncertainty of new ones will hurt already weakened business sentiment," Chua says. He notes, however, the exemption for drugmakers building a production facility in the U.S. may mitigate the blow. Singapore hosts nine of the world's top 10 drugmakers, with major pharma firms already establishing or expanding manufacturing operations in the U.S., Chua notes.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0405 ET - President Trump's drug tariffs will have a limited impact on big Swiss pharma players given their U.S. presence, Vontobel analysts write. While there is broad room for interpretation of Trump's announcement, Roche and Novartis are shielded by their U.S. footprints, while Sandoz should be unaffected since it focuses on exempted generic products, the analysts say. Galderma's exposure is less clear as it has a smaller U.S. presence. Tariffs will increase U.S. drug prices and supply concerns, but they shouldn't threaten the country's leading position in drug development, Vontobel says. Galderma shares fall 3.2%, with Roche and Novartis down 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. Sandoz shares trade broadly flat. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0337 ET - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0239 ET - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0041 ET - The U.S.'s fresh tariffs on pharmaceutical imports aren't a big concern for China's healthcare sector, according to Jefferies analysts in a research note. Chinese pharmaceutical companies have minimal sales exposure to the U.S. market, the analysts say. Investors may have concerns regarding Chinese biotechs' U.S. expansion, but at this stage, most companies are at least 1-2 years away from potential U.S. commercialization, they note. Also, most Chinese biotechs have U.S. partners and are immune to the tariffs, so they barely dent China's healthcare powerhouses, the analysts add. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2232 ET - IHH Healthcare is expected to post steady 2H earnings before stronger growth in 2026 on strength in its key regions, Affin Hwang IB analyst Andrew Lim says in a note. Singapore continues to be IHH's biggest earnings driver, with growth in 2026 supported by Mount Elizabeth Hospital reopening its beds. Payor pressures in Malaysia may cap margins, but India is set to deliver the fastest growth, supported by Fortis's bright prospects and aggressive five-year bed expansion plan, he says. Lim is positive on IHH's outlook amid rising inpatient demand, stable margins and a demand-driven expansion, supported by growing healthcare awareness. Affin Hwang maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR8.10. Shares are 0.8% higher at MYR7.43. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2141 ET - Malaysia's healthcare sector is poised for sustained long-term growth, driven by rising noncommunicable diseases and an aging population, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. Private hospital bed capacity is expected to grow 4%-5% annually through 2028, narrowing the gap with developed markets, he says. A government campaign to attract foreign travelers is set to boost healthcare tourism, reinforcing long-term sector growth, he adds. The sector's near-term challenges include manpower shortages and regulatory uncertainty, but private operators like IHH Healthcare and KPJ Healthcare are better positioned to attract talent. CIMB maintains an overweight rating on the sector, with IHH Healthcare as its top pick due to its diversified assets. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1934 ET [Dow Jones]--Volatility in Telix Pharmaceuticals's share price is set to continue — this time for positive reasons. That's the view of Bell Potter after Telix secured transitional pass through, or TPT, status for its Gozellix imaging agent for prostate cancer in the U.S. "The decision to pursue this strategy (for a refresh on the pass through) has proven to be a master stroke by the company and one that has shown its major competitor in the PSMA imaging market asleep at the wheel," analyst John Hester says. TPT status on Gozellix is effective Oct. 1. Bell Potter assumes an average reimbursement of $5,000 per patient. It retains a buy call on Telix and A$23.00/share price target. Telix ended Thursday at A$15.38. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0850 ET - Switzerland plans to give UBS Group seven years to comply with tougher capital requirements, as the country's government detailed the timing of implementation of an update to its "too big to fail" regulatory regime. "The government points out that the 7-year period is depending on the parliamentary progress which may suggest that the end date is fixed," RBC Capital Markets says in a note. The Swiss Federal Council on Friday said a requirement to increase UBS's capital buffer will start in 2028 and be gradually implemented until the bank can fully back its foreign subsidiaries by the end of 2034. The government launched a consultation on the plan that is due to end on Jan. 9, 2026. UBS shares gain 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0341 ET - Moshi Moshi Retail is expected to maintain its market leadership despite rising competition, supported by differentiated products, efficient sourcing and a large store network, Maybank Securities says. Analyst Suttatip Peerasub projects gross margin improvement in 2025-2026 from higher-margin products and better supplier terms as production scales. Maybank forecasts 3Q net profit growth of 13% and sales growth of 15% on solid same-store sales and store additions, with full-year earnings projected to rise 19% in 2025 and 16% in 2026. Moshi also deserves a premium valuation given its three-year EPS CAGR of 16.7%, more than double the sector average of 7.6%, Peerasub adds. Maybank maintains a buy rating and THB51.00 target on Moshi's stock, which was recently at THB40.75.(jason.chau@wsj.com)

0040 ET - Morgan Stanley analysts think that some observers are missing the size of the likely second-half skew in Macquarie's earnings. Their forecast for 6% first-half earnings growth significantly lags behind consensus, but they think that analysts elsewhere aren't seeing the full picture. They say in a note that a recent client compensation agreement and risks of green-energy asset write-downs could also weigh on profit. However, they think that a recovery in global capital markets and commodities revenues should drive a powerful second-half recovery. They are looking for 12% earnings growth across the full year, and their annual net profit forecast is broadly in line with consensus. MS stays equal-weight on the stock and lifts its target price by 4.6% to A$226.00. Shares are up 0.4% at A$216.82. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1912 ET [Dow Jones]--Fonterra Shareholders' Fund sets a high bar for future earnings, prompting Macquarie to raise its price target on the dairy cooperative by 17% to NZ$8.61/share. Fonterra has agreed to the sale of its consumer businesses, known as Mainland Group, to Lactalis for NZ$3.85 billion. It expects to take three years to replace the hit to operating profit from that deal. Fonterra is targeting 5% annual Ebit growth to FY 2028, the bank says. Its new price target reflects the Mainland sale and higher sustainable earnings implied in the Ebit target, although it is less bullish about this goal than management. Fonterra is up 1.3% at NZ$7.91 today. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1517 ET - Crude futures close the week higher, spurred by continuing Ukrainian strikes against Russian oil infrastructure and the prospect of additional EU and U.S. sanctions that raise concerns of supply constraint. WTI settles up 1.1% at $65.72 a barrel, a 5.3% weekly gain and highest close since early August. Executives polled Sept. 10-18 by the Dallas Fed lowered their average year-end WTI price expectation to $63.06 a barrel from $68.18 in the 2Q survey. Responses ranged from $50 to $80 a barrel, with 43% seeing the year-end price between $60 and $64.99, and 33% between $65 and $69.99. Brent settles up 1% at $70.13 a barrel, up 5.2% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1354 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. increased by six to 424 in a fifth straight weekly rise, and was 60 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. In the Dallas Fed's 3Q survey of energy companies, 42% of exploration and production executives said they have slightly delayed investment decisions because of oil price uncertainty or cost of production, and 36% said they have significantly delayed decisions. On regulatory changes made since January, 57% said they had reduced break-even costs for new wells by less than $1 a barrel, and 25% estimated reductions between $1 and $1.99 a barrel. Rigs directed at natural gas this week slipped by one to 117, or 18 more than a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1011 ET - Oil futures are higher on rising geopolitical risk premium with continuing Ukraine attacks on Russian oil facilities,and President Trump's rekindling of tariff battles with duties on pharmaceuticals and heavy trucks. Although most of the buying has been by short-sided traders liquidating their positions, "the crude market has gained merit on steady demand seemingly keeping up with added supplies from OPEC+," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. The latest tariffs may signal to India and China "that additional tariffs could be heading their way if they remain buyers of Russian crude," he adds.WTI is up 0.8% at $65.48 a barrel and Brent gains 0.6% to $69.83. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0911 ET - Canada GDP is on track on for 0.8% annualized growth in 3Q following the release of July GDP data, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP by industry climbed 0.2% in July from the prior month, powered by Canada's oil-and-gas sector, marking the first month-over-month increase since March. But the Statistics Canada says early indicators suggest GDP was flat in August, which "suggests that the economy stalled again that month," Grantham says. The Bank of Canada forecast a 1% annualized gain for 3Q. Grantham says the BOC will likely lean on the pending employment and inflation data before deciding on whether to cut rates again on October 29. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0332 ET - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

1917 ET [Dow Jones]--Carnarvon Energy now owns 18-19% of Strike Energy's equity following the latter's capital raising, effectively securing a blocking stake in the Western Australia-focused natural- gas producer. That means Carnarvon "is now integral in any commercial negotiations if Hancock was seeking to fully consolidate the Perth basin Kingia acreage," says Macquarie. Hancock bought 50% of the West Erregulla natural-gas field in 2023 for A$450 million. Strike Energy owns the other 50% of West Erregulla. Macquarie resumes coverage of Strike Energy with a neutral call and A$0.10/share price target. Strike Energy ended Thursday at A$0.105. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1503 ET - Canada's main stock market in Toronto has enjoyed a stellar year, with year-to-date gains now exceeding 20% after three quarters. National Bank Financial says Toronto stocks have benefited from a rush into gold, as traders deal with persistent inflation uncertainty fueled by the Trump administration's tariffs and USD depreciation. Gold stocks have surged, and NBF calculates that gold stocks account for 11% of the market capitalization of Toronto-listed equities, or the highest share since an 11.5% peak in 2012. The historical average is 4.7% based on records dating back to the mid-1970s. NBF says it expects the share of market capitalization of gold stocks to surpass the 2012 peak as gold remains attractive as an alternative asset. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1035 ET - Silver futures are pushing towards highs last seen in April 2011, according to data from FactSet. The most-active contract is now up 1.8% to $45.91 a troy ounce. While gold has dominated headlines, analysts are also looking at strong gains elsewhere. "It has been silver and platinum that have delivered the most eye-catching returns," says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank in a note. Industrial demand has been a factor underpinning strength in silver, he adds, along with investors seeking the metal as an alternative to gold. "The metal's high-beta nature has amplified inflows from investors seeking leverage to gold's record-breaking run," says Hansen. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0232 ET - Lotte Chemical's earnings could start to recover in 2028 after completing its business restructuring, KB Securities analysts Wooje Chun and Yunju Song write in a note. The South Korean company is still reeling from the worst-ever global chemical-industry downturn, and is expected to complete its ongoing business consolidation by 2027, they say. The analysts note that global naphtha cracking center operators in Europe, South Korea, China and Japan shut down some of their facilities for four years until 2027 to address the oversupply. KB expects Lotte Chemical's operating loss to narrow to KRW287.5 billion in 2026. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0053 ET - The price of copper "will need to rise dramatically" to spur enough investment in new mines to meet demand, Shaw and Partners analysts say in a note. Bringing on new copper supply is challenging and production costs are likely to rise, say the analysts. "Expansions of existing mines are becoming increasingly expensive, and new projects often involve lower-grade ore and higher development costs," they say. "This is compounded by the slow pace of new discoveries and stricter environmental and social standards, creating a bottleneck that prevents a rapid increase in supply to meet growing demand." Shaw expects copper to average $10,524/metric ton next year, versus consensus of $9,786/ton and spot of $10,287/ton. The broker's key copper stock picks are AIC Mines, Aurelia Metals and Sunstone Metals. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2212 ET - Liontown Resources provides some additional disclosures on its FY 2025 results call, including that it expects capital expenditure of A$55 million-A$70 million from FY 2028 onward, Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon says in a note. That compares to Citi's expectation of roughly A$80 million a year, says McCutcheon. Liontown also flags flat depreciation and amortization in FY 2026 year over year, before a moderation in FY 2027, she adds. Liontown's FY 2025 revenue is in line with expectations, and Citi makes only minor changes to its estimates following the results, McCutcheon says. Citi has a sell rating on Liontown. It raises its target to A$0.50 from A$0.45. Liontown is up 6.1% at A$0.96. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2128 ET - Geo Energy Resources stands to benefit from the construction of a 92-kilometer hauling road and a jetty in Indonesia's South Sumatra, Phillip Securities Research's Paul Chew says in commentary. There is no change to the projected completion and commissioning by mid-2026, with an additional two months needed to become fully operational. Once completed, coal production at the Singapore-listed Indonesian company's TRA mine in South Sumatra is expected to increase to 12 million metric tons in 2027 from 2 million metric tons in 2025. The brokerage raises its target price on the stock to S$0.59 from S$0.47 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 1.1% higher at S$0.455. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2120 ET - Growth in the rare-earth supply chain outside of China depends on manufacturers and governments being receptive to higher prices for rare earths produced elsewhere, Morgan Stanley analyst Rahul Anand says in a note. Anand's remarks follow a Reuters report that says G-7 nations and the European Union are considering price floors to encourage production. Already, the U.S. has set a price floor for some light rare earths in a deal with MP Materials. Australian officials say they are also considering price floors for critical minerals. MS remains overweight on Australia's Lynas and Iluka "as key beneficiaries of higher ex-China rare earths prices," says Anand. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1230 ET - American Airlines won't be able to stop the city of Chicago from awarding rival United Airlines five additional gates at O'Hare, a Cook County Circuit Court judge has ruled. Both airlines claim O'Hare a hub, but American has accused United of trying to squeeze it out and hampering American's expansion plans. It said Friday that the ruling would give United a competitive advantage in Chicago, but said it remains committed to the city. United President Brett Hart told employees that the decision was a "powerful validation of United's growth in the city. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

0857 ET - CarMax seems to be investing more on lowering prices but that hasn't led to a recovery in market share or same-store sales, which is a worrying sign, say JPMorgan analysts, who cut the stock's price target to $50 from $65. It suggests a slippery slope that could be hard to recover from and that pressures both gross profit per unit and volumes. Over the last five years, CarMax has made significant investments in omni-channel and sourcing functions but has been unable to gain share. "The business has not been agile enough to adjust to volatile industry backdrops despite prior experiences," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0851 ET - Truck customers are likely to wait and see how new U.S. tariffs influence pricing, so expect orders and demand to take a hit in the near term, Jefferies analysts write. If prices increase across the industry in response, demand will likely be ht in the fourth quarter and into 2026, they add. Trump plans to impose 25% tariffs on heavy-duty trucks, but it isn't known how vehicles with parts made in the U.S., or which are USMCA-compliant, will be treated. Jefferies estimates that Daimler Truck and Traton could be at a 300-600 basis point cost disadvantage to manufacturers that assemble most vehicles in the U.S., such as Volvo and Paccar. Daimler Truck shares fall 2.3%, Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.4%. Paccar shares rise 6.7% pre-market. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0832 ET - - President Trump's new tariffs on trucks put around 18% of Daimler Truck's 2026 group profit at risk and 9% of Traton's, according to Bank of America Securities estimates. Daimler Truck currently manufactures around 60% of its U.S. sales in Mexico, with Traton at 65%, making them particularly vulnerable, analysts at the bank say. In practice, the bank expects both companies to offset tariffs by moving some production into the U.S. and raising prices, with 6% price increase probably enough to fully offset the impact. By contrast, 100% of Volvo's North American truck production is within the U.S. "We think this positions the company well to boost margins and/or market share." Daimler Truck shares fall 2.5% and Traton shares are down 2% while Volvo shares rise 3.7%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0830 ET - Despite a sizable 2Q earnings miss, CarMax's business model remains solid, but its recovery will take time amid increasing competition and weaker demand, say Oppenheimer analysts, who downgrade the stock to perform. Repositioning efforts could be disruptive in the short-term, the analysts say, adding that investors are unlikely to bet on the stock without clear recovery signals. "We are now increasingly concerned that the company must endure a potentially elongated strategic retrenchment, focused upon restrengthening and marketing better core consumer propositions, all the while contending with a potentially weakening demand backdrop," the analysts say. CarMax is 1% lower premarket after sliding 20% Thursday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0822 ET - CarMax's 2Q across-the-board miss reveals that it's also losing market share, Truist says, cutting the price target to $47 from $74. Its competitor Carvana is growing rapidly and "has gained material mindshare with customers focused on an online experience," analyst Scot Ciccarelli says. Additionally, compared with CarMax, traditional dealers "have deeper inventory pools of specific makes/models, appealing to customers that have already narrowed-down their choices," he says. The company also continues to struggle with its price perception among consumers, according to Ciccarelli. CarMax is off 1% premarket after plunging 20% Thursday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0422 ET - Horizon Robotics' top-up share placement could be a slight surprise for investors, say DBS Group Research analysts in a note. The Chinese technology company is raising HK$6.38 billion via a placement. The analysts note the "relatively short" three months since a previous placement of around US$600 million and a rich net cash reserve of 15.5 billion yuan as of end-1H. Nonetheless, DBS thinks this move is reasonable for the company, which provides smart-driving solutions for vehicles, as the share price has risen 34% since its 1H results release and is hovering near an all-time high. DBS trims its target to HK$11.00 from HK$11.50 but maintains a buy rating on Horizon Robotics, which closed 8.5% lower at HK$9.70. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0348 ET - Bernstein initiates coverage on Rheinmetall's stock with a market-perform rating and a target price of 1,960 euros. The bank believes that the German arms maker will continue to win orders in the coming years, as demand outstrips supply and early capacity increases drive the growth. "But after the first phase of replenishment and during the transition to long-cycle investments, we worry a perfect storm in short-cycle (armoured vehicles, shells) products." Bernstein says that a transition to a long-cycle portfolio will be long and costly. Analysts expect more attractive risk-rewards for players that are already positioned, at cheaper valuations. Bernstein's top pick is BAE Systems. Rheinmetall shares are down 0.3% at 1,972.50 euros. (alistair.macdonald@wsj.com)

0339 ET - Safran will get a boost from Turkish Airlines' Boeing aircraft purchase, as the French manufacturer produces the Leap engine for the 737 MAX and equipment for the 787 plane, Jefferies analysts say. Turkish Airlines said Friday that it decided to purchase a total of 75 aircraft from Boeing, consisting of 50 firm and 25 option orders for 787-9 and 787-10 models with deliveries scheduled between 2029 and 2034. Negotiations with Boeing regarding the purchase of a total of 150 aircraft, consisting of 100 firm and 50 option orders for the 737-8/10 MAX models, have been completed, the carrier said. Safran shares are up 1.2% at 295.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0318 ET - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0252 ET - The Philippine port sector's outlook appears positive, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The ports' container volumes gained strong momentum in 1H and are poised to quicken in 2H, the analyst says. Growth is anchored by resilient domestic consumption and a pickup in trade activity, with foreign containers accounting for the majority of the ports' traffic, the analyst says. International Container Terminal Services, via Manila International Container Terminal port's dominant gateway role, remains best-placed to capture this momentum, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of PHP590.00 on International Container Terminal Services shares, which are 0.6% lower at PHP495.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: A Milestone of Hope
data.description:
NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / September 26, 2025 / Originally published on Aflac Newsroom

What comes to mind when you think of a bell? Perhaps it elicits memories of school days spent waiting for one to ring, or maybe you're reminded of the Liberty Bell that symbolizes independence.

Bells have long represented important transitions and, in many cases, freedom. For cancer patients, it's no different - the bell is a beacon of hope that echoes a sound of victory. For patients at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta, it also marks the start of a new chapter to be filled with more time to simply be a kid. In its simplicity, the ringing of the bell transforms the end of cancer treatment into more than a medical milestone - it becomes a celebration of life, courage and the extraordinary strength of a child.

Take 4-year-old Austin, for example. He has a condition called adrenoleukodystrophy, a rare genetic condition affecting the nervous system and adrenal glands, which doctors have been monitoring since he was an infant. After learning to navigate the challenges of this disease, Austin and his family came to the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children's Healthcare of Atlanta for answers that would explain Austin's repeat illnesses and infections. That's when they discovered that Austin - who was just 2 years old at the time - also has B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. After two years, Austin rang the end-of-treatment bell in May together with friends, family and the Cherokee County Fire Department, who have been by his side this entire journey.

Another special moment was with Noelle, who also recently completed her treatment journey. After fainting during what seemed to be a normal breakfast with her family, Noelle was rushed to her pediatrician for lab work. The next morning, her family received a call that changed everything - she was being sent to the emergency room because her bloodwork showed signs of leukemia. Noelle was admitted to the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center after being diagnosed with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. After more than two years of active treatment consisting of countless medications and procedures, Noelle proudly rang the bell at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center, marking her new beginning.

Since 1995, Aflac agents, employees and The Aflac Foundation, Inc. have given more than $191 million to The Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center, a national leader among childhood cancer, hematology and blood and marrow transplant programs. Behind these donations is a team of people cheering on each patient, which is why Aflac recently added an alert to their intranet that pops up every time a child rings the bell at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center. Now, every time a child completes their treatment and participates in the coveted tradition of ringing the bell, thousands of Aflac employees will get a notification, allowing them to take part in the celebration virtually. And even though they won't know patients' names or circumstances, the hope is that each time they're notified that the bell has been rung, the Aflac family will be reminded that their support - big or small - is making a difference in the lives of children with cancer.

Learn more about how Aflac is building a bridge to brighter futures for children like Austin and Noelle by visiting Aflac.com/BridgeToBrighter.
